1: “My son, be attentive to my wisdom; incline your ear to my understanding.” The chapter’s opening call to wisdom-hearing introduces the warning against the strange woman — the wisdom-versus-folly contrast in sexual form. Christ addressed the sexual ethics of the heart directly: “I say to you that everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart” (Matthew 5:28), going deeper than Proverbs’ behavioral warning to the source of the behavior. Christ’s wisdom penetrates where the father’s instruction cannot follow — into the heart.
2: “That you may keep discretion, and your lips may guard knowledge.” The kept discretion and guarded knowledge of wisdom’s way are the opposite of the dripping-honey lips of the strange woman (v3). Christ is the one who speaks truth, life, and discretion: “the words that I have spoken to you are spirit and life” (John 6:63). His lips do not drip seductive flattery but speak the hard truth that leads to life; his words guard those who receive them.
3: “For the lips of a forbidden woman drip honey, and her speech is smoother than oil.” The sweet-seductive speech of the strange woman is the counterfeit of wisdom’s true speech. Peter’s description of false teachers mirrors Proverbs’ warning: “with bold faces they will exploit you” (2 Peter 2:3); Jude 12: “hidden reefs at your love feasts, feasting with you without fear.” Against all false and seductive speech, Christ sets his own: “no one ever spoke like this man” (John 7:46) — distinctive, authoritative, and life-giving.
4: “But in the end she is bitter as wormwood, sharp as a two-edged sword.” The reversal from sweetness to bitterness, from smoothness to cutting sharpness, is the trajectory of all false paths. Hebrews 4:12 applies the two-edged sword image to the word of God itself — “sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing to the division of soul and of spirit” — but there the sharpness serves discernment and life. The strange woman’s sword-sharpness wounds to death; Christ’s sword-word cuts for healing and truth.
5: “Her feet go down to death; her steps follow the path to Sheol.” The descent to Sheol that follows the strange woman’s path becomes, in the NT, the descent that Christ undertook to rescue those already there. Ephesians 4:9-10 reads the Psalms’ language of Christ’s descent: “he also descended into the lower regions, the earth.” Acts 2:27 cites Psalms 16:10: “you will not abandon my soul to Hades.” The feet that go down to Sheol in Proverbs describe the path of sin; Christ descended to that same Sheol to lead captives out (Ephesians 4:8).
6: “She does not ponder the path of life; her ways wander, and she does not know it.” The strange woman’s unconscious wandering — not pondering the path of life, not knowing she wanders — is the condition of those who need the shepherd. Luke 15:4: “what man of you, having a hundred sheep, if he has lost one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine in the open country, and go after the one that is lost, until he finds it?” Christ comes to seek those who wander without knowing it — the lost sheep of a house that does not know the way of life.
7: “And now, O sons, listen to me, and do not depart from the words of my mouth.” The urgent renewal of the call to hear — “now” (weʿattāh) — reflects the earnestness of wisdom at every point. Christ’s own urgent calls to hear carry this same register: “the hour is coming and is now here” (John 5:25); “unless you repent, you will all likewise perish” (Luke 13:3). The wisdom tradition’s urgency is the earthly form of the eschatological urgency that Christ brings into the present moment.
8: “Keep your way far from her, and do not go near the door of her house.” The spatial language of danger-avoidance — stay far, do not come near — frames temptation as a domain to be avoided rather than a battle to be fought at close quarters. Hebrews 12:2 points to the wisdom of fixing one’s gaze on Christ rather than on the temptation: “looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith.” Distance from the temptation is achieved by proximity to Christ — “flee youthful passions and pursue righteousness, faith, love, and peace” (2 Timothy 2:22).
9: “Lest you give your honor to others and your years to the merciless.” The strange woman steals the young man’s honor and years — his strength, dignity, and life. Christ restores what sin strips away: “I came to give abundant life” (John 10:10); the prodigal son who spent his inheritance in a foreign country receives back robe, ring, and feast from the father (Luke 15:22-24). The honor stripped by folly is restored by the grace of the Father who runs to meet the returning son.
10: “Lest strangers take their fill of your strength, and your labors go to the house of a foreigner.” The exploitation of strength and labor by strangers/foreigners echoes the Deuteronomic curse: “a nation that you do not know shall eat up the fruit of your ground and of all your labors” (Deuteronomy 28:33). Christ reverses this curse: Galatians 3:13 — “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law.” The strength and labor that belong to the one who is his are not consumed by a pitiless stranger but become the material of the new creation that the Spirit cultivates within.
11: “And at the end of your life you groan, when your flesh and body are consumed.” The end-of-life groaning of the one who spent his years in folly is the experiential endpoint of the path that began at the strange woman’s door. Paul uses similar groaning language for the unredeemed condition of the created order and the human body: “we ourselves groan inwardly as we wait eagerly for adoption as sons, the redemption of our bodies” (Romans 8:23). The groan of regret becomes, through Christ, the groan of hopeful anticipation — the same word transformed by the resurrection promise.
12: “And you say, ‘How I hated instruction!’” The deathbed realization that instruction was despised captures the tragic self-knowledge that comes too late. Christ’s parable of the rich man and Lazarus (Luke 16:19-31) presents exactly this end-of-life insight arriving too late for remedy. The compassionate invitation of the wisdom teacher — “come to me and learn” (Matthew 11:28-29) — is the open door before which the one who despised instruction stands. The “how I hated instruction” lament is the background against which Christ’s gracious invitation stands in relief.
13: “I did not listen to the voice of my teachers or incline my ear to my instructors.” The failure to hear the teachers is the individual instance of Israel’s structural failure to hear the prophets: “I sent to you all my servants the prophets, sending them persistently, but you did not listen” (Jeremiah 29:19). Christ stands at the end of that prophetic line as the Son: “he had still one other, a beloved son. Finally he sent him to them, saying, ‘They will respect my son’” (Mark 12:6). The failure to hear the teachers culminates in the crucifixion of the Son.
14: “I am at the brink of utter ruin in the assembled congregation.” The near-ruin of the man overtaken by folly in the midst of the covenant community — the public, communal dimension of shame — corresponds to the shame Christ bore publicly: “enduring the cross, despising the shame” (Hebrews 12:2). Christ bore the covenant community’s judgment on the covenant-breaker publicly, outside the gate (Hebrews 13:12), so that those at the brink of ruin might be brought back within the congregation through his substitution.
15: “Drink water from your own cistern, flowing water from your own well.” The positive counterpart to the strange woman’s seduction is the covenant marriage relationship — exclusive, refreshing, life-giving. Ephesians 5:25-27 applies the marriage relationship directly to Christ and the church: Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her. The cistern-and-well imagery of faithful marriage becomes, in the NT, the picture of Christ’s own exclusive covenant love for his bride-community (Revelation 21:2: the new Jerusalem as a bride adorned for her husband).
16: “Should your springs be scattered abroad, streams of water in the streets?” The rhetorical question — implying the answer “no” — concerns the exclusive channeling of marital love to the covenant wife. Christ’s love for the church is not scattered but precisely directed: he gave himself “for her” (Ephesians 5:25). Yet paradoxically, the living water Christ gives flows outward from his people to all: “out of his heart will flow rivers of living water” (John 7:38) — the exclusive covenant fidelity produces an abundant outward overflow rather than a contained private resource.
17: “Let them be for yourself alone, and not for strangers with you.” The exclusivity of the marriage covenant — the wife and her love belong to one husband — is the temporal image of Christ’s exclusive covenant with his people. 2 Corinthians 11:2 uses the betrothal image: “I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ.” The “strangers” who threaten the exclusivity of the covenant marriage correspond to the false gospels and seductions that threaten to draw the church away from her exclusive devotion to Christ.
18: “Let your fountain be blessed, and rejoice in the wife of your youth.” The blessing of the marriage fountain and the rejoicing in the covenant wife are positive commands that correspond to Christ’s own delight in his people. Zephaniah 3:17: “he will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing.” The husband’s rejoicing in the wife of his youth is the temporal image of the divine rejoicing over the covenant community — and ultimately over the bride purified by Christ for the Father’s pleasure.
19: “A lovely deer, a graceful doe — let her breasts fill you at all times with delight; be intoxicated always in her love.” The extravagant language of delight in covenant marriage — intoxicated always with love — matches the language of Song of Solomon and points toward the NT’s marriage metaphor for Christ and the church. Revelation 19:7-9: “the marriage of the Lamb has come, and his Bride has made herself ready... Blessed are those who are invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb.” The exclusive delight of the faithful husband anticipates the eternal feast of mutual joy between Christ and his bride.
20: “Why should you be intoxicated, my son, with a forbidden woman and embrace the bosom of an adulteress?” The rhetorical “why?” addresses the absurdity of choosing folly when wisdom’s delight is available — the same question that underlies the NT’s “how can we who died to sin still live in it?” (Romans 6:2). The availability of Christ — “for freedom Christ has set us free” (Galatians 5:1) — makes the return to folly not merely foolish but inexplicable in light of what has been given.
21: “For a man’s ways are before the eyes of YHWH, and he ponders all his paths.” Divine omniscience of all human paths — YHWH sees and weighs each step — establishes the theological ground for moral accountability. Hebrews 4:13: “no creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account.” Christ is identified as the one “to whom we must give account” — the judge whose seeing surpasses all concealment and before whom every path stands naked.
22: “The iniquities of the wicked ensnare him, and he is held fast in the cords of his sin.” Sin as self-binding cords — the sinner trapped by his own transgressions — describes the condition that Christ comes to release: “if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed” (John 8:36). Isaiah 61:1 — “to proclaim liberty to the captives” — is the programmatic statement that Jesus applies to himself at Nazareth (Luke 4:18-19). The cords of sin that bind the wicked are what the cross severs for those united to Christ by faith.
23: “He dies for lack of discipline, and because of his great folly he is led astray.” The death of the undisciplined fool — led astray by his own folly — is the condition that makes the incarnation necessary. Christ came “to seek and to save the lost” (Luke 19:10); the sheep “led astray” by folly is precisely the one for whom the shepherd searches (Matthew 18:12). The death-for-lack-of-discipline is answered by the one who, through suffering, learned obedience (Hebrews 5:8) and can now be “the source of eternal salvation to all who obey him” (Hebrews 5:9).
